1: bayyôm hahûʾ — "in that day" — the eschatological day of YHWH. ʿîr ʿāz lānû — "we have a strong city" — in contrast to Isaiah 24:10's qiryat-tōhû (city of chaos). yĕšûʿâ yāšît ḥômôt wāḥēl — "he sets salvation as walls and ramparts" — the city's security is not military but theological; yĕšûʿâ (salvation) is the structural material of the city's defense.
2: pitḥû šĕʿārîm — "Open the gates!" — the imperative of welcome. gôy ṣaddîq šōmēr ʾĕmûnîm — "a righteous nation that keeps faith/faithfulness" (ṣaddîq, H6662; ʾĕmûnîm, plural of faithfulness). The city is open to those who are righteous and faithful — the entry requirement is character shaped by YHWH's covenant, not ethnic identity.
3: yēṣer sāmûk tiṣṣōr šālôm šālôm — "the steadfast mind you keep in perfect peace" (yēṣer, H3336, formed/framed inclination or formed mind; sāmûk, H5564, upheld/leaned upon). The doubled šālôm šālôm (peace, peace) is an intensified form — "complete peace" or "deep peace." The basis is trust (bāṭûaḥ bĕkā, trusting in you) — the orientation of the mind determines the quality of peace received.
4: biṭḥû ḇYhwh ʿăḏê-ʿaḏ — "trust in YHWH forever and ever" — ʿăḏê-ʿaḏ is the strongest Hebrew expression of perpetual duration. The reason: kî bĕYāh Yhwh ṣûr ʿôlāmîm — "for in Yah YHWH is the Rock of Ages/everlasting Rock." The title ṣûr ʿôlāmîm combines the rock-refuge imagery (Psalms 18:2; Isaiah 31:3) with the eternal duration of divine character — the reliability of YHWH is permanent by definition.
5: kî hišpîl yōšĕbê mārom — "for he brings low those who dwell on high" — the divine reversal pattern. The lofty city (qiryâ niśśāʾâ, the exalted city) is brought down; it is laid low to the ground (lāʾāreṣ yiggāʿennāh), brought even to the dust (ʿad-ʿāpār). The vocabulary of Isaiah 26:5 reverses the vocabulary of self-exaltation from Isaiah 14:13-14 — pride goes to dust.
6: The foot of those who were afflicted (regelyānî ûpăʿămê ḏallîm) treads on what was the proud city. The inversion: those at the bottom of the social order now walk on the ruins of what oppressed them. This is not mere social revolution but the specific reversal that characterizes YHWH's governance — the poor (ḏal) and the needy are elevated precisely as the powerful are cast down.
7: ʾōraḥ laṣṣaddîq mĕšārîm — "the path of the righteous is level/smooth" (mĕšārîm, H4339, from yāšar, straight/even). mêšārîm tipal-maʿgal ṣaddîq — "you make smooth/level the track of the righteous." YHWH is the one who clears the path — the righteous do not walk on their own strength but on a way prepared for them.
8: ʾap bĕderek mišpāṭêkā Yhwh qiwwînûkā — "in the path of your judgments/justice, YHWH, we wait for you" — the waiting (qāwâ, the same root as Isaiah 25:9) is done on the path of divine justice, not in withdrawal from the world. lĕšimkā ûlĕzikrekā taʾăwat nāpeš — "for your name and your memory/remembrance is the desire of the soul" — the ultimate object of longing is the name and presence of YHWH.
9: napšî ʾiwwitîkā ballaylâ — "with my soul I have desired you in the night" — the night as the time of intense spiritual seeking (cf. Psalms 63:6). ʾap rûḥî bĕqirbî ʾăšaḥărêkā — "even with my spirit within me I seek you earnestly" (the šaḥar root means to seek at dawn). kî ʾăšer mišpāṭêkā lāʾāreṣ ṣedeq lāmĕdû yōšĕbê ṯēḇēl — "for when your judgments fall on the earth, the inhabitants of the world learn righteousness" — divine judgment has a pedagogical purpose.
10: yûḥan rāšāʿ bal-lāmad ṣedeq — "when grace is shown to the wicked, he does not learn righteousness" — the inverse pedagogical principle: unmerited favor does not reform character the way discipline does. The wicked continue to deal wrongly even in an upright land (bĕʾereṣ nĕkōḥôt yĕʿawwēl) and do not see YHWH's majesty (gĕʾût Yhwh bal-yirʾeh).
11: Yhwh rāmâ yādĕkā — "YHWH, your hand is lifted high" — the extended hand as the instrument of judgment (cf. Isaiah 5:25; Isaiah 9:12, 17, 21; Isaiah 10:4). The adversaries do not see it (bal-yĕḥĕzāyûn) but will be ashamed. ʾēš ṣārêkā tōʾkĕlēm — "fire shall consume your adversaries" — the fire of divine jealousy/zeal (qinʾat ʿamkā) is what destroys the enemies of YHWH's people.
12: Yhwh tišpāt šālôm lānû — "YHWH, you will ordain/appoint peace for us" — the passive reception of peace as YHWH's gift. kî gam kol-maʿăśênû pāʿaltā llānû — "for indeed all our works you have accomplished for us" — the acknowledgment that even the good works of the community are ultimately YHWH's doing; the monergistic theology of grace here anticipates Paul's formulation in Philippians 2:13.
13: ʾădōnāy ʾĕlōhênû bĕʿālûnû ʾădōnîm zûlatekā — "O YHWH our God, lords other than you have ruled over us" — the historical confession of unfaithfulness and subjugation to foreign powers. raq-bĕkā nazkîr šĕmekā — "but through you alone do we acclaim your name" — the exclusive focus on YHWH as the only name worth calling upon emerges from the history of failed alternatives.
14: mētîm bal-yiḥyû rĕpāʾîm bal-yāqûmû — "the dead will not live; the shades/departed spirits will not rise" — a specific group (the oppressive lords of Isaiah 26:13) will not experience resurrection; their memory is destroyed (lakēn pāqadtā wattašmîdēm). This is in deliberate contrast with Isaiah 26:19 where YHWH's dead will rise — the distinction between those under divine judgment (no resurrection) and those under divine covenant (resurrection).
15: yāsaptā laggôy Yhwh yāsaptā laggôy — "you have added to the nation, O YHWH, you have added to the nation" — the repeated verb emphasizes the extension of Israel through the ingathering of exiles and gentile converts. YHWH is glorified (nikbadtā) and has extended all the borders of the land. The double accent on increase/addition is the opposite of the diminishment of the corrupt city (Isaiah 26:5-6).
16: Yhwh bĕṣar pĕqādûkā — "YHWH, in distress they sought you" (pāqad, to seek out, attend to — here the people attend to God precisely when in trouble). ṣāqûn laḥaš mûsar — "they poured out a whispered/quiet prayer for discipline" — the word laḥaš (H3908) connotes whispering, quiet prayer, perhaps muttered under duress. Distress drives the community to YHWH when prosperity does not.
17: kĕmô hārâ taqrîḇ lālēdet tāḥîl tizʿaq bĕḥeblêhā — "like a pregnant woman approaching delivery who writhes and cries out in her labor pains" — the simile for the community's condition before YHWH. The birth metaphor is carefully developed: the pain is real, the outcome is expected, but the next verse reveals the tragic surprise.
18: The tragedy: hārînû ḥalnû kĕmô yālādnû rûaḥ — "we were with child, we writhed in pain, but we gave birth to wind/nothing" (rûaḥ here means wind, emptiness). No salvation has been accomplished on earth (lōʾ naʿăśeh ṣĕdāqôt bāʾāreṣ); the inhabitants of the world have not fallen (wĕbal-yippĕlû yōšĕbê ṯēḇēl). Human striving and even prophetic promise has produced nothing — only YHWH's intervention (Isaiah 26:19) can bring the dead to life.
19: yiḥyû mētêkā nĕbēlātî yāqûmûn — "your dead will live; my corpses/body (nĕbēlāh) will arise" — an explicit resurrection declaration, directly countering Isaiah 26:14,18. hāqîṣû wĕrannĕnû šōkĕnê ʿāpār — "awake and sing, you who dwell in the dust" — the reversal of death-sleep into singing. ṭal ʾôrōt ṭallekā — "for your dew is a dew of light" — resurrection is connected to the dew that preserves life in arid seasons; ṭal ʾôrōt (dew of lights) may image the resurrection glory.
20: lĕkā ʿammî bōʾ bĕḥăḏāreykā ûsĕgōr dĕlatōtêkā baʿăḏekā — "come, my people, enter your rooms/chambers, and shut your doors behind you" — the hiding of God's people during the moment of judgment echoes the Passover (Exodus 12:22-23: stay inside, blood on the doorposts) and the Flood (Noah shut in the ark). ḥăbî kimaṭ-rageʿ ʿad-yaʿăbōr-zāʿam — "hide for a little while until the wrath has passed."
21: kî-hinnēh Yhwh yōṣēʾ mimĕqōmô — "for behold, YHWH is coming out of his place" — the decisive divine self-disclosure in judgment. lipqōd ʿăwōn yōšēb hāʾāreṣ ʿālāyw — "to punish the iniquity of the earth's inhabitants against him." The earth will disclose (tigalleh) its blood — the unjust blood shed throughout history will be exposed and the earth will no longer cover (wĕlōʾ tĕkasseh ʿôd) it. Justice will be complete.
